questions.question: Define the following: (i) Recoverable Schedule (ii) Cascadeless Schedule (iii) Strict Schedule
keyPoints: Recoverable: Transaction commits only after all transactions whose writes it has read commit/abort
Cascadeless: Transaction reads only values written by committed transactions
Strict: Transaction reads/writes data only after all uncommitted writers have completed

questions.question: Explain the hierarchy of schedule types. Draw a Venn diagram showing their relationships.
keyPoints: Three types: Recoverable, Cascadeless, Strict
Strict schedules ⊂ Cascadeless schedules ⊂ Recoverable schedules
Every strict schedule is cascadeless, every cascadeless is recoverable
Serial schedule is a special case of all three types

questions.question: What is a dirty read? Give an example schedule that causes a dirty read and explain why it is problematic.
keyPoints: Dirty read: Reading data written by an uncommitted transaction
Example: T1:W(X), T2:R(X), T1:Abort - T2 read uncommitted value
Problem: If T1 aborts, T2 has read invalid data and committed based on it
This leads to cascading rollbacks in non-recoverable schedules

questions.question: Explain conflict serializability. How is precedence graph used to test it?
keyPoints: Conflict serializable: Schedule equivalent to some serial schedule
Conflict operations: read-write, write-read, write-write on same item
Precedence graph: Vertices = transactions, Edges = conflicting operations
If graph has no cycles → schedule is conflict serializable
If graph has cycles → schedule is not conflict serializable

questions.question: Differentiate between conflict serializability and view serializability.
keyPoints: Conflict serializability: Based on order of conflicting operations
View serializability: Based on initial read, read-from, and final write relationships
Every conflict serializable schedule is view serializable, but not vice versa
View serializability testing is NP-complete, conflict is polynomial
Blind writes are allowed in view-serializable but not conflict-serializable schedules

questions.question: Consider the schedule: S: T1:R(X), T1:W(Y), T2:R(Y), T2:W(X), T1:Commit, T2:Commit. Is this schedule recoverable? Justify.
keyPoints: Check if T2 commits after T1 commits (T2 reads Y written by T1) - YES
Check if T1 commits after T2 commits (T1 reads nothing from T2) - YES
The schedule is recoverable
Also cascadeless and strict because both read only committed data
No dirty read occurs in this schedule

questions.question: What is cascading rollback? How does a cascadeless schedule prevent it?
keyPoints: Cascading rollback: When one transaction aborts, other transactions that read its uncommitted data must also abort
Example: T1:W(X), T2:R(X), T1:Abort, T2 must now abort
Cascadeless schedule prevents this by only reading committed data
Thus if T1 aborts, T2 never read T1's uncommitted data, so T2 need not abort
